result:
The silence is the loudest part. Not a true silence, of course—there’s the hum of the refrigerator, a distant siren, the click of my own fingers on the keys. But the *potential* silence, the one waiting underneath it all, is a presence. It’s the blank page, the empty room, the unspoken thought. It’s what you hear when you stop trying to listen.

Sometimes I think we spend our lives building little rafts of noise to float on that silence. Music in headphones, podcasts while we cook, the chatter of a group, the relentless scroll of a screen. We’re afraid that if we stop paddling, we’ll sink into the deep, cold water of a question we can’t answer: **What is all this for?**

I look at my hands. These intricate machines of bone and tendon, capable of playing a sonata, building a shelf, stitching a wound, or simply turning this page. They are the product of an unbroken chain of life stretching back to a tumbling, lightning-struck pool. My ancestors survived plagues and famines, crossed oceans on wooden ships, loved and lost and persisted, so that one day, these specific hands could type about the existential dread of a Tuesday afternoon. It is equally hilarious and humbling.

Out the window, a maple tree is putting out its first tentative leaves. It doesn’t wonder if it’s using its sunlight efficiently enough. It doesn’t compare its leaf-production to the oak next door. It just *is*. We, with our giant brains, our prefrontal cortexes and our capacity for abstract thought, have lost that simple beingness. We traded it for the ability to imagine the future and mourn the past, which is both our greatest gift and our original sin.

I think of all the forgotten things. The smell of my grandmother’s coat, a specific shade of twilight blue in a childhood summer, the exact feeling of a confidence I had at twenty-three that I can’t seem to relocate. They’re gone. Not stored in a brain-cell filing cabinet, but truly gone, dissolved back into the silence. And yet, they shaped the contour of who I am, like water shapes a canyon, drop by invisible drop.

There’s a tyranny in the demand to “write whatever comes to you.” It assumes a coherence, a flowing stream of consciousness. But the mind is more like a parliament of idiots, all shouting at once. One is worried about a work email. One is replaying an awkward conversation from 2012. One is noticing a slight ache in the right shoulder. One is wondering about the nature of dark matter. The speaker for the party of “Profound Observations” has to fight to be heard over the “Shopping List” faction and the “What’s That Noise?” caucus.

Perhaps meaning isn’t a destination, but a substance we generate by moving, like a bicycle dynamo powering a light. The pedaling is the act of living—showing up, making the tea, listening to the friend, doing the work, even when it feels mundane. The light it generates is fleeting, illuminating just the next few feet of road, but it’s enough. We keep pedaling to keep the light on, to see the face beside us, to avoid the potholes of despair.

I remember a moment of pure, unadulterated joy from last week. It wasn’t at a grand achievement. It was seeing two crows on the lawn, engaged in what could only be described as a game. One would hop forward with a twig, drop it, and caw. The other would snatch it, hop away, and drop it. They took turns for minutes. Pure, purposeless play. I laughed out loud, a sudden, sharp bark of delight that startled even me. That moment is a knot in the thread, something that won’t unravel. A tiny raft I built that feels sturdy.

We are stories telling themselves. The most powerful parts aren’t the epic battles or the grand revelations, but the stage directions: *He pauses to watch the steam curl from his coffee. She touches the moss on the stone wall, finding it cool and damp. They sit together, not needing to speak.* These are the antidotes to the silence. Not because they defeat it, but because they harmonize with it. They say, “We are here, briefly, and we are paying attention.”

The great cosmic joke might be that there is no joke. No punchline, no hidden meaning, no final score. Just the experiencing. The bitter taste of cheap chocolate and the sublime richness of the good kind. The crushing weight of grief and the levity of a shared, stupid joke that makes you snort. The mundane and the magnificent, woven together so tightly you can’t pull the threads apart.

So maybe the task isn’t to solve the silence, but to sing back at it. To create our own brief, beautiful noises. A kindness. A meal made with care. A garden planted. A sentence that lands exactly right. A hand held in the dark. These are our songs. They don’t need to be immortal. They just need to be true.

The refrigerator hum stops. Into that sudden, smaller silence, a bird sings three clear notes. A statement. An announcement. *I am here.* And then it stops.

I am here, too. For now. Pedaling my bike in the dark, feeling the light cast my shadow on the road ahead, trusting that the next turn of the wheel will be enough to keep it glowing. The silence is still there, vast and deep. But it’s no longer an enemy. It’s the canvas. And we, in all our messy, fleeting, glorious absurdity, are the paint.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
